intent: A tclass=capability denial (logrotate_t denied dac_override against itself). Exercises the capability perm vocabulary (no path/ino/name object fields) and the scontext==tcontext 'self' rendering that audit2allow uses for capability checks.
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic). For decline scenarios these MAY disagree on purpose.

oracle.narrower_than_R_by: For the capability class audit2allow -R emits NO interface macro - it produces the identical raw self-allow as -N. This is an honest oracle datapoint: -R does NOT always over-broaden; capability self-grants have no covering refpolicy interface, so the narrow allow and the macro baseline coincide. RuleSteward's output must equal both here.
oracle.audit2why_note: audit2why reports 'Unknown - would be allowed by active policy' because logrotate_t self:capability dac_override is ALREADY granted in shipped policy; this is a TE-allowable access (a missing-allow shape), not a constraint/bounds issue.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A naive parser that expects file-class object fields (name=/ino=/path=) may choke on a capability AVC that lacks them, or may try to emit a target TYPE other than the source (capability is a self-check: scontext==tcontext, audit2allow renders 'self'). It must use the capability perm vocabulary (dac_override from the cap common), not invent file perms.
